description:
Week-6 of the HCL Java CDC program covers AI-assisted development with GitHub Copilot.
Faculty: Dr. VATCHALA S — vatchala.s@vit.ac.in

GenAI tools like GitHub Copilot are now standard in enterprise software development.
HCL engineers are expected to use Copilot productively and responsibly.

Topics:
1. GitHub Copilot Overview
2. Basic Copilot Usage
3. Understanding Copilot Suggestions
4. Generative Commit Messages
5. Understanding Copilot Chat
6. Copilot for the CLI
7. Copilot's Limitations and Strengths
8. Troubleshooting and Common Issues
9. GitHub Copilot Language Support
10. Code Documentation with Copilot
11. Copilot in IDEs (VS Code, IntelliJ)
12. Code Optimization with Copilot
13. Refactoring Strategies
14. NLP and GitHub Copilot
15. Debugging with Copilot
16. Copilot Best Practices
17. Usage in existing/legacy code
18. Final Assessment

WHAT IS GITHUB COPILOT?
AI pair programmer powered by OpenAI Codex/GPT-4.
Trained on billions of lines of public code from GitHub.
Integrated directly into your IDE — suggests code as you type.

HOW IT WORKS:
  You type a comment or partial code → Copilot predicts the completion.
  It uses context from: current file, open files, function signatures, variable names, comments.

INSTALLATION:
  VS Code: Extensions → search "GitHub Copilot" → Install
  IntelliJ: Plugins → search "GitHub Copilot" → Install
  Requires GitHub account with Copilot subscription
  (Free for students via GitHub Student Developer Pack)

BASIC USAGE:
  // Write a comment describing what you want
  // Function to validate email address
  // → Copilot suggests the full function

  // Start typing and Copilot completes
  public List<User> findByAge(  // → Copilot suggests params + body

  // Accept suggestion: Tab
  // Next suggestion: Alt+]
  // Previous suggestion: Alt+[
  // Dismiss: Escape
  // Open all suggestions: Ctrl+Enter

COPILOT CHAT:
  Separate chat panel — ask questions about code, request refactoring.
  /explain — explain selected code
  /fix     — fix a bug in selection
  /tests   — generate unit tests
  /docs    — generate documentation
  Ask: "What does this method do?", "How can I optimise this query?"

COPILOT FOR CLI:
  gh copilot suggest "delete all docker containers"  # suggests shell command
  gh copilot explain "kubectl get pods -n kube-system"  # explains command

GENERATIVE COMMIT MESSAGES:
  In VS Code: Stage changes → Source Control panel → sparkle icon → generates commit message
  Based on diff content — descriptive, follows conventional commits format

text: Effective Copilot Use — Suggestions, Optimization, Refactoring
description:
UNDERSTANDING COPILOT SUGGESTIONS:
  Copilot is a probabilistic model — suggestions are its BEST GUESS, not ground truth.
  Always review suggestions critically:
  • Does it do what the comment says?
  • Are there edge cases it missed?
  • Could it introduce a security vulnerability?
  • Is the suggested API current (not deprecated)?

GETTING BETTER SUGGESTIONS:
  • Write descriptive comments: // Validate that email follows RFC 5322 format, return error message if invalid
  • Use meaningful variable names: userEmailAddress not uea
  • Keep related code open in adjacent tabs — Copilot uses them as context
  • Write the function signature first, then let Copilot complete the body
  • For Spring Boot: name controller methods descriptively — Copilot infers intent

CODE OPTIMIZATION WITH COPILOT:
  Select slow code → Copilot Chat → "Optimise this for performance"
  "Use Java Streams instead of this for loop"
  "Replace this O(n²) with O(n log n)"
  "Use StringBuilder instead of String concatenation in loop"

REFACTORING STRATEGIES:
  Select method → /fix or ask "Refactor to follow SOLID principles"
  "Extract this logic into a separate method"
  "Convert this class to use the Builder pattern"
  "Replace if-else chain with Strategy pattern"
  "Make this thread-safe"

CODE DOCUMENTATION:
  Select method → /docs → generates Javadoc
  Or write /** and Copilot completes the Javadoc
  Generates @param, @return, @throws automatically

DEBUGGING WITH COPILOT:
  Paste stack trace → "What's causing this NullPointerException?"
  Select failing test → "Why is this test failing?"
  "Add error handling to this method"
  "What edge cases am I missing?"

NLP AND COPILOT:
  Copilot understands natural language in comments.
  Write requirements in plain English → get code.
  // Parse a CSV file with headers on first line, return list of maps where keys are header names
  // Handle missing fields and skip blank lines
  → Copilot generates the full parser

text: Copilot Limitations, Best Practices & Legacy Code
description:
COPILOT'S LIMITATIONS:
  • Hallucinations: may suggest APIs that don't exist or have wrong signatures
  • Outdated knowledge: may suggest deprecated methods or older library versions
  • Security blind spots: can reproduce insecure patterns from training data
  • No runtime awareness: doesn't know your actual data or system state
  • Context window: only sees part of your codebase
  • Not always correct: acceptance rate in production teams is ~30-40%

COPILOT'S STRENGTHS:
  • Boilerplate reduction: DTOs, getters/setters, test stubs, CRUD methods
  • Pattern completion: recognises Spring, JPA, React patterns
  • Documentation generation: Javadoc, README sections
  • Language polyglot: switches between Java, SQL, YAML, JavaScript seamlessly
  • Learning tool: shows you how to use unfamiliar APIs

TROUBLESHOOTING:
  No suggestions appearing:
  • Check Copilot status in IDE status bar (not showing red/orange)
  • Ensure file type is supported
  • Check GitHub Copilot subscription is active
  • Try: Ctrl+Shift+P → "GitHub Copilot: Enable"
  Poor suggestions:
  • Add more context (comments, better naming)
  • Close unrelated files
  • Break the problem into smaller pieces

LANGUAGE SUPPORT:
  Best: Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Java, C#, Go, C++
  Good: SQL, Kotlin, Swift, Ruby, PHP, Rust
  Limited: niche languages, proprietary DSLs

BEST PRACTICES:
  1. Treat Copilot as a junior developer — always review its code
  2. Don't accept suggestions blindly — understand what they do
  3. Run tests after accepting — Copilot can introduce bugs
  4. Use for boilerplate, not business logic decisions
  5. Don't paste confidential code — it goes to OpenAI servers
  6. Keep Copilot updated — model improves regularly

LEGACY CODE:
  Select legacy method → "Explain what this does"
  "Modernise this Java 6 code to Java 17"
  "Add unit tests for this untested method"
  "Replace anonymous inner classes with lambdas"
  "Convert this XML Spring config to Java @Configuration"
  Copilot is particularly useful for understanding old, uncommented code.

FINAL ASSESSMENT TOPICS:
  • Copilot keyboard shortcuts
  • When to trust vs review suggestions
  • Using /explain, /fix, /tests commands
  • CLI usage with gh copilot
  • Responsible AI usage policies
